title: Timothy: A Faithful Messenger
speaker: Dr. John Battle

outline.title: Introduction: Background and Setting
subpoints: Paul writes from Corinth during his second missionary journey, to the church he started in Thessalonica
Paul had three companions at various points: Timothy, Silas, and Luke (the doctor, who stayed in Philippi)

Tracing the journey: Acts 17:14 — Timothy and Silas stayed in Berea while Paul went to Athens; 1 Thessalonians 3:1-2 — Paul sent Timothy from Athens back to Thessalonica; Acts 18:5 — Timothy and Silas rejoined Paul in Corinth
Paul's concern for the persecuted young church he had to leave so suddenly made him send Timothy back
Proverbs 13:17 and 25:13: a faithful messenger brings healing and refreshes the soul of his masters

outline.title: Timothy's Purpose (vv. 1-3a)
subpoints: 'We could no longer endure it' (v. 1) — Paul was not just saying the church couldn't endure it; he personally couldn't live with not knowing how they were doing
Illustration: the nervous parent with the first babysitter — Paul's anxiety about the Thessalonians drove him to send help
Two purposes: to help Paul (so he could carry on in Corinth without distraction) and to strengthen the Thessalonians
Two words: 'establish you' (to make you stand) and 'encourage you' (same root as Holy Spirit / Paraclete)
Leaders standing strong under persecution strengthen the whole community; without them, each person must stand alone — much harder for new believers

outline.title: Timothy's Qualifications (v. 2)
subpoints: Biblical knowledge from childhood: Timothy knew the Scriptures, taught by his mother and grandmother (2 Timothy 3:15)

Trial-tested: Timothy witnessed Paul being stoned at Lystra on the first missionary journey (Acts 14), lying in the road bleeding, then rising and walking back into the city — this formed Timothy's expectations of what serving Christ costs
Well spoken of: spoken well of by the brothers when Paul first took him as a companion on the second journey
Reliance on the Lord: Timothy was 'God's fellow worker' (better manuscripts) — the same phrase Paul uses in 1 Corinthians; you work, God works, and you work together

outline.title: Timothy's Message (vv. 3b-5)
subpoints: 'That no one should be shaken by these afflictions' (v. 3)

Two things about the Thessalonians' sufferings: (1) they were appointed by God — God had ordained it; (2) they were predicted — 'we told you before that we would suffer tribulation, just as it happened'
When suffering comes, do not blame the people doing it — realize God has ordained it for his reasons; those people, in their hatred and sin, God will deal with
Trials are ordained by God, predicted by God, and ongoing (v. 4: 'just as it happened, and as you know')
But the message included comfort: 'to establish and encourage you in your faith' — the same encouragement the Holy Spirit provides
Hebrews 10: when we gather on the Lord's Day, we are to encourage one another — we are messengers of God's comfort to each other

summary: A morning lecture by Dr. John Battle on 1 Thessalonians 3:1-5 — the first of two lectures covering the chapter. The background is traced through Acts and 1 Thessalonians: Paul writes from Corinth during his second missionary journey, having sent Timothy back to Thessalonica from Athens out of deep anxiety for the persecuted young church he had to abandon. Three aspects of Timothy as messenger are examined: his purpose (to comfort Paul's heart and to establish and encourage the Thessalonians facing persecution); his qualifications (biblical knowledge from childhood, trial-tested by witnessing Paul's stoning at Lystra, well spoken of, and reliant on the Lord as 'God's fellow worker'); and his message (that their sufferings were appointed by God, predicted in advance, and ongoing — combined with the comfort to stand firm). The sermon is addressed in part to those who are relatively comfortable, as preparation for possible future persecution. Application: we are to be messengers of God's comfort and strength to one another, just as Timothy was.
transcript:
## Introduction: Background and Setting

Such a pleasure to preach here. I started something years ago when I first came — I began preaching from the book of 1 Thessalonians and I just thought, well, I'll just keep doing that when I come down. Later we're going to get to the second coming of Christ in chapters four and five, where there is especially an emphasis and of course a lot of controversy on eschatology and interpretation of the rapture. I figured at the rate I'm going, and how long it's taking, by the time I get to it, we'll all know the answers to these questions.

But today we are looking at chapter 3 of 1 Thessalonians, and one advantage of preaching through a book like this is that you see topics come up that you probably normally wouldn't cover if you were just picking things out. Today is one of those — it's Timothy as a messenger being sent to the church by Paul. This passage is rich and wonderful and full of applications.

Today in the morning message I'll cover verses 1 through 5, and then in the afternoon message cover verses 6 through 10 — it breaks up nicely, because verses 1-5 emphasize Timothy himself as the messenger, and the afternoon will look at the actual message he brought back from the church to the apostle.

Three parts this morning: Timothy's purpose, his qualifications, and his message — the message he carried from Paul to the church.

The book of Proverbs has something to say about messengers. Proverbs 13:17: 'A wicked messenger falls into trouble, but a faithful envoy brings healing.' And Proverbs 25:13: 'Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest is a faithful messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the soul of his masters.' As Paul sent Timothy, he refreshed Paul's soul because Timothy was a faithful carrier of the message.

**Background: Tracing the Journey**

Paul wrote this letter to the church in Thessalonica while he was in the city of Corinth during his second missionary journey. He had at various points three companions: Timothy, Silas, and Luke, who joined the group when they were still in the province of Asia and then stayed behind in Philippi after the adventurous visit there.

Three men continued on to Thessalonica, where Paul started a church in about three or four weeks — Jews who believed from the synagogue and many Gentiles who joined them. This young church was immediately persecuted by Jewish leaders and others (Acts calls them 'certain rude fellows of the baser sort'). The new leaders told Paul to leave. He was taken south to Berea and then to Athens.

From Acts 17:14: Timothy and Silas stayed in Berea while Paul went to Athens; they were supposed to come and join him with all speed. 1 Thessalonians 3:1-2: Paul says he sent Timothy from Athens back to Thessalonica. Then from Acts 18:1 and 18:5: Paul went to Corinth alone, and Timothy and Silas rejoined him there, bringing the good news from Macedonia. This letter was written as Paul was with those two companions in Corinth.

## Timothy's Purpose (vv. 1-3a)

Paul says in verse 1: 'We could no longer endure it.' Not that the church couldn't endure it — but he, Paul, personally couldn't live with not knowing how they were doing. If you've ever hired your first babysitter and left your precious child with someone you have questions about — you made a list of phone numbers, you thought about calling every hour — you understand something of what Paul felt. He couldn't bear it.

So he sent Timothy for two reasons. First, so that Paul himself could carry on in Corinth without being constantly distracted by worry about the Thessalonians. Second — and this is more important — to strengthen the Thessalonians themselves.

Two words in verse 2 describe Timothy's purpose: 'to establish you' — literally, to make you stand — and 'to encourage you.' That word *encourage* is related to the same word as the Holy Spirit, the Paraclete, the Comforter. So Paul sent Timothy to do what the Holy Spirit does: comfort, encourage, make stand.

When leaders are present under persecution, the whole community is strengthened. When the leader is gone, each individual must stand alone — much more difficult for those who are new at being Christians and have never been tested like this before. I think of that old movie Quo Vadis, where Peter, hobbling back to Rome, is greeted in the dungeon by chains rattling and then rejoicing: 'Peter is here!' The whole scene changes. That is the role Timothy played in Thessalonica.

## Timothy's Qualifications (v. 2)

First: **biblical knowledge from childhood**. We know from Paul's letters to Timothy that from childhood he knew the Holy Scriptures, taught by his mother Eunice and grandmother Lois. You cannot be a faithful messenger without knowing the message. The dedicated teachers in our Sunday school classes, the parents who bring up their children in the knowledge of the Lord — this is what produced Timothy.

Second: **trial-tested**. Timothy lived in the city of Lystra, and in Paul's first missionary journey, Paul preached there, was at first hailed as a god, then dragged out of the city and stoned. The disciples gathered around his body. That young man Timothy was in that group. He saw the Apostle Paul lying in the road with wounds and bruises from the rocks — and then saw him rise up by the Lord's strength and walk back into the city. Timothy saw what it cost to serve Christ and was willing to pay it.

Third: **well spoken of** — this is mentioned when Paul first takes Timothy as his companion on the second journey.

Fourth: **reliance on the Lord**. Verse 2 calls Timothy 'God's fellow worker in the gospel of Christ' (better manuscripts). Paul uses this same phrase elsewhere — in 1 Corinthians: 'we are fellow workers with God.' You are doing the work. God is doing it through you. You work and God works and you work together. Timothy knew that everything he did was effective not because he was such a great communicator, but because God was working through him.

## Timothy's Message (vv. 3b-5)

His message had two aspects: a statement about suffering and a comfort.

The statement about suffering had two parts. First, their sufferings were **appointed by God**: 'you yourselves know that we are appointed to this' (v. 3). God has ordained this. God put it in his appointment book. When something bad happens, we should not get mad at the people doing it — ultimately God has ordained it for his reasons. The people doing it in their hatred and sin God will deal with, but it is God who has appointed it to happen.

Second, their sufferings were **predicted**: 'for in fact we told you before, when we were with you, that we would suffer tribulation, just as it happened, and you know' (v. 4). Paul says: don't be surprised. I told you before. This is what Jesus said too — in the passages we saw in Sunday school, he said through many tribulations we enter the kingdom of God. You've heard this before.

And third, their sufferings were **ongoing** — 'just as it happened, and as you know.'

But the message wasn't just a statement about suffering — it included comfort. Verse 2: 'to establish you and encourage you in your faith.' That same word: Paraclete, Comforter.

Hebrews 10 says that when we gather together on the Lord's Day, we are to encourage one another. We need to be messengers of God's comfort and strength in times of affliction. Let us pray.
